author: Augustine of Hippo

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

title: Chapter XIII. 21–26.

source.title: NPNF1-07. St. Augustine: Homilies on the Gospel of John; Homilies on the First Epistle of John; Soliloquies

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: This short section of the Gospel, brethren, we have in this lesson brought forward for exposition, as thinking that we ought also to say something of the Lord’s betrayer, as now plainly enough disclosed by the dipping and holding out to him of the piece of bread.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: Of that indeed which precedes, (namely), that Jesus, when about to point him out, was troubled in spirit, we have treated in our last discourse; but what I perhaps omitted to mention there, the Lord, by His own perturbation of spirit, thought proper to indicate this also, that it is necessary to bear with false brethren, and those tares that are among the wheat in the Lord’s field until harvest-time, because that when we are compelled by urgent reasons to separate some of them even before the harvest, it cannot be done without disturbance to the Church.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: Such disturbance to His saints in the future, through schismatics and heretics, the Lord in a way foretold and prefigured in Himself, when, at the moment of that wicked man Judas’ departure, and of his thereby bringing to an end, in a very open and decided way, his past intermingling with the wheat, in which he had long been tolerated, He was troubled, not in body, but in spirit.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: For it is not spitefulness, but charity, that troubles His spiritual members in scandals of this kind; lest perchance, in separating some of the tares, any of the wheat should also be uprooted therewith.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: “Jesus,” therefore, “was troubled in spirit, and testified, and said: Verily, verily, I say unto you, that one of you shall betray me.”

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: “One of you,” in number, not in merit; in appearance, not in reality; in bodily commingling, not by any spiritual tie; a companion by fleshly juxtaposition, not in any unity of the heart; and therefore not one who is of you, but one who is to go forth from you.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: For how else can this “one of you” be true, of which the Lord so testified, and said, if that is true which the writer of this very Gospel says in his Epistle, “They went out from us, but they were not of us; for if they had been of us, they would no doubt have continued with us”?

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: Judas, therefore was not of them; for, had he been of them, he would have continued with them.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: What, then, do the words “One of you shall betray me” mean, but that one is going out from you who shall betray me?

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: Just as he also, who said, “If they had been of us, they would no doubt have continued with us,” had said before, “They went out from us.”

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: And thus it is true in both senses, “of us,” and “not of us;” in one respect “of us,” and in another “not of us;” “of us” in respect to sacramental communion, but “not of us” in respect to the criminal conduct that belongs exclusively to themselves.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: “Then the disciples looked one on another, doubting of whom He spake.”

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: For while they were imbued with a reverential love to their Master, they were none the less affected by human infirmity in their feelings towards each other.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: Each one’s own conscience was known to himself; but as he was ignorant of his neighbor’s, each one’s self-assurance was such that each was uncertain of all the others, and all the others were uncertain of that one.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: “Now there was leaning on Jesus’ bosom, one of His disciples, whom Jesus loved.”

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: What he meant by saying “in His bosom,” he tells us a little further on, where he says, “on the breast of Jesus.”

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: It was that very John whose Gospel is before us, as he afterwards expressly declares.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: For it was a custom with those who have supplied us with the sacred writings, that when any of them was relating the divine history, and came to something affecting himself, he spoke as if it were about another; and gave himself a place in the line of his narrative becoming one who was the recorder of public events, and not as one who made himself the subject of his preaching.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: Saint Matthew acted also in this way, when, in coming in the course of his narrative to himself, he says, “He saw a publican named Matthew, sitting at the receipt of custom, and saith unto him, Follow me.”

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: He does not say, He saw me, and said to me.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: So also acted the blessed Moses, writing all the history about himself as if it concerned another, and saying, “The Lord said unto Moses.”

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: Less habitually was this done by the Apostle Paul, not however in any history which undertakes to explain the course of public events, but in his own epistles.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: At all events, he speaks thus of himself: “I knew a man in Christ fourteen years ago, (whether in the body, or whether out of the body, I cannot tell: God knoweth;) such an one caught up into the third heaven.”

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: And so, when the blessed evangelist also says here, not, I was leaning on Jesus’ bosom, but, “There was leaning one of the disciples,” let us recognize a custom of our author’s, rather than fall into any wonder on the subject.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: For what loss is there to the truth, when the facts themselves are told us, and all boastfulness of language is in a measure avoided?

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: For thus at least did he relate that which most signally pertained to his praise.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: But what mean the words, “whom Jesus loved”?

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: As if He did not love the others, of whom this same John has said above, “He loved them to the end” (ver.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: 1); and as the Lord Himself, “Greater love hath no man than this, that a man lay down his life for his friends.”

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: And who could enumerate all the testimonies of the sacred pages, in which the Lord Jesus is exhibited as the lover, not only of this one, or of those who were then around Him, but of such also as were to be His members in the distant future, and of His universal Church?

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: But there is some truth, doubtless, underlying these words, and having reference to the bosom on which the narrator was leaning.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: For what else can be in dicated by the bosom but some hidden truth?

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: But there is another more suitable passage, where the Lord may enable us to say something about this secret that may prove sufficient.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: “Simon Peter therefore beckons, and says to him.”

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: The expression is noteworthy, as indicating that something was said not by any sound of words, but by merely beckoning with the head.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: “He beckons, and says;” that is, his beckoning is his speech.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: For if one is said to speak in his thoughts, as Scripture saith, “They said [reasoned] with themselves;” how much more may he do so by beckoning, which expresses outwardly by some sort of signs what had previously been conceived within!

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: What, then, did his beckoning mean?

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: What else but that which follows?

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: “Who is it of whom He speaks?”

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: Such was the language of Peter’s beckoning; for it was by no vocal sounds, but by bodily gestures, that he spake.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: “He then, having leaned back on Jesus’ breast,”—surely the very bosom of His breast this, the secret place of wisdom!—“saith unto Him, Lord, who is it?

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: Jesus answered, He it is to whom I shall give a piece of bread, when I have dipped it.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: And when He had dipped the bread, he gave it to Judas Iscariot, the son of Simon.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: And after the bread, Satan entered into him.”

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: The traitor was disclosed, the coverts of darkness were revealed.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: What he got was good, but to his own hurt he received it, because, evil himself, in an evil spirit he received what was good.

verses.book: Lectures or Tractates on the Gospel According to St. John.
verses.text: But we have much to say about that dipped bread which was presented to the false-hearted disciple, and about that which follows; and for these we shall require more time than remains to us now at the close of this discourse.
